name: Grape wine (food medium)
basis: per 100 g (~100 mL; finished dry red table wine, wine density ~0.99 g/mL). concentration_mM computed on a per-litre basis.

components.name: water
components.source: USDA FDC 173190 (SR Legacy, red table wine)

components.name: ethanol
components.evidence: Alcohol, ethyl 10.54 g/100 g (~105 g/L; the defining carbon/energy source of wine).

components.name: glycerol
components.source: Nieuwoudt et al., Glycerol in South African table wines (277 wines): mean 10.49 g/L dry red / 6.82 g/L dry white
components.evidence: Second most abundant carbon compound after ethanol; ~10.5 g/L in dry red wine.

components.name: L-tartaric acid
components.source: AWRI Technical Review 221 (Wilkes 2016), 277 wines: tartaric mean 2.70 g/L (1.0-7.3)
components.evidence: Dominant wine organic acid; grape-derived, not metabolised by yeast/LAB. MISSING from FooDB record.

components.name: L-malic acid
components.source: AWRI Technical Review 221 (Wilkes 2016): malic mean 1.27 g/L (<0.1-4.2)
components.evidence: Substrate of malolactic fermentation; lower in reds post-MLF, higher in whites. MISSING from FooDB record.

components.name: succinic acid
components.source: AWRI Technical Review 221 (Wilkes 2016): succinic mean 1.15 g/L (<0.1-3.1)
components.evidence: Yeast fermentation by-product. MISSING from FooDB record.

components.name: L-lactic acid
components.source: AWRI Technical Review 221 (Wilkes 2016): lactic mean 0.80 g/L (<0.1-3.3)
components.evidence: Product of malolactic fermentation; higher in reds. MISSING from FooDB record.

components.name: acetic acid
components.source: AWRI Technical Review 221 (Wilkes 2016): acetic mean 0.40 g/L (<0.1-1.7)
components.evidence: Dominant volatile acid. MISSING from FooDB record.

components.name: citric acid
components.source: AWRI Technical Review 221 (Wilkes 2016): citric mean 0.12 g/L (<0.1-1.4)
components.evidence: Minor grape acid, often added. MISSING from FooDB record.

components.name: D-fructose
components.source: USDA FDC 173190 total sugars 0.61 g/100 g; split per wine chemistry (Wine Folly / winemaking literature: fructose > glucose in finished dry wine as glucose ferments first)
components.evidence: Residual hexose; fructose-dominant at fermentation end.

components.name: D-glucose
components.source: USDA FDC 173190 total sugars 0.61 g/100 g; split per wine chemistry
components.evidence: Residual hexose; largely consumed, lower than fructose in finished wine.

components.name: L-proline
components.source: Reviews of wine amino acids: proline 300-1300 mg/L in red wine (30-85% of total AA); grape must proline mean 697 mg/L
components.evidence: Dominant free amino acid; poorly consumed by yeast so it persists. Range 30-130 mg/100 g.

components.name: L-arginine
components.source: Wine amino acid reviews: arginine second most abundant, largely consumed in fermentation (must ~388 mg/L, residual ~50-150 mg/L)
components.evidence: Major yeast-assimilable nitrogen source; partly consumed.

components.name: total protein / amino nitrogen
components.evidence: Protein 0.07 g/100 g; mostly free amino acids (proline, arginine, alanine, glutamate/glutamine, GABA) plus peptides.

components.name: copper
components.evidence: Cu ~0.024 mg/100 g (rounds to 0.0 in some panels).

components.name: vitamin B1 (thiamine)
components.evidence: Thiamin ~0.003-0.005 mg/100 g (largely consumed by yeast).

components.name: acetaldehyde
components.evidence: Genuine yeast fermentation carbonyl; present, unquantified here (typically 10-100 mg/L).

components.name: methanol
components.evidence: Genuine (pectin demethylation); ~0.1 g/L in red wine, higher than white. Unquantified here.

components.name: D-gluconate
components.evidence: Genuine minor acid, elevated in botrytised grapes; unquantified.

components.name: pyruvate
components.evidence: Genuine glycolytic intermediate, low mg/L; unquantified.

components.name: L-arabinose
components.evidence: Non-fermentable pentose from grape; present, low; unquantified.

components.name: ammonium
components.evidence: Residual assimilable nitrogen; largely consumed in fermentation; unquantified.

components.name: sulfate
components.evidence: Genuine anion (also from SO2 additions); unquantified.

remove.name: Cholesterol
remove.reason: Animal sterol; biologically implausible in a grape/yeast fermentation product. FooDB predicted, not measured. USDA reports 0 mg cholesterol in wine.

remove.name: Retinol
remove.reason: Preformed (animal) vitamin A; absent in wine. USDA reports 0 vitamin A activity. FooDB grape-carryover artifact.

remove.name: Beta-Carotene
remove.reason: Grape-skin carotenoid essentially absent in finished wine (USDA vitamin A ~0); FooDB grape-derived prediction, not a growth nutrient of wine.

remove.name: Lutein
remove.reason: Grape carotenoid negligible in finished wine; FooDB grape-carryover, not a measured wine nutrient.

remove.name: Phytomenadione
remove.reason: Vitamin K1; USDA reports ~0 vitamin K in wine. FooDB grape-carryover, negligible.

remove.name: Iron (Fe3+)
remove.reason: Redundant duplicate speciation of iron; consolidated into single 'iron' entry (USDA Fe 0.46 mg/100 g).

remove.name: Fe2+ mitochondria
remove.reason: Compartment-tagged duplicate of iron from a model namespace; not a distinct wine nutrient.

remove.name: O2 O2
remove.reason: Dissolved oxygen is near-zero in finished/bottled wine (reductive storage); not a defining medium component.

missing_added: D-glucose and D-fructose split with amounts
quantified USDA vitamin and mineral panel
keep_uncovered: Malvidin, Malvidin 3-glucoside and acyl derivatives (dominant red-wine anthocyanin)
Catechin, Quercetin, Quercitrin, Myricetin, Isorhamnetin, Astragalin (flavonoids)
Caffeic acid, Caftaric acid & glutathionyl-caftaric acids, Gallic acid, Ferulate, Gentisic acid, Neochlorogenic acid (hydroxycinnamic/benzoic acids)
Piceatannol, Astringin, delta-Viniferin, Vitisin A, Hydroxytyrosol (stilbenes/phenols)
Genuine phytochemicals with no BiGG metabolite; retained as uncovered, not growth substrates
notes: Dominant carbon/energy sources are ethanol (~105 g/L) then glycerol (~10.5 g/L); dominant organic acid is tartaric (~2.7 g/L) with malic/succinic/lactic/succinic and acetic secondary. The FooDB record was missing every major wine organic acid (tartaric, malic, succinic, lactic, acetic, citric) - the key curation gain here. Residual sugars are low (dry wine, ~6 g/L total, fructose>glucose). Proline is the dominant free amino acid. Values are for a dry red table wine (USDA FDC 173190); white/sweet styles differ (more malic, less lactic; higher residual sugar).
